dialogues.title: Morning Coffee Greeting
dialogues.context: At a small café in Funchal, a customer greets the barista.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Bom dia! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Good morning! Everything OK?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia! Tudo bem, obrigado. E você?
lines.translation: Good morning! Everything OK, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Tudo bem, sim. P'ra já!
lines.translation: Everything OK, yes. Right away!

dialogues.title: Neighbours Meeting
dialogues.context: Two neighbours meet in the street in the afternoon.

lines.speaker: Vizinha A
lines.text: Boa tarde, Maria! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Good afternoon, Maria! Everything OK?

lines.speaker: Vizinha B
lines.text: Boa tarde, Dona Fátima! Tudo bem, pois é. E a senhora?
lines.translation: Good afternoon, Mrs. Fátima! Everything OK, indeed. And you?

lines.speaker: Vizinha A
lines.text: Tudo bem, querida. Tenha uma boa tarde!
lines.translation: Everything OK, dear. Have a good afternoon!

roleplay.scenario: You enter a small shop in Madeira to buy something. The shopkeeper greets you.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Tudo bem?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Everything OK?

options.text: Bom dia! Tudo bem, obrigado. E o senhor?
options.translation: Good morning! Everything OK, thank you. And you?
options.feedback: Excellent! You used a polite greeting and asked back.
Good, but a bit informal for a first greeting in a shop. You could also ask how they are.
That's a goodbye! You need to respond to their 'Tudo bem?' first.

nodes.npc_text: Tudo bem, obrigado. Em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Everything OK, thank you. How can I help you?

options.feedback: You successfully completed the greeting loop! Now you can ask for what you need.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, tudo bem. Em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, everything OK. How can I help you?

options.feedback: You've responded, but next time try to ask back 'E o senhor?' to be more polite.

nodes.npc_text: Ah... Bom dia para si também.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah... Good morning to you too.

options.feedback: It's okay! Remember to respond to 'Tudo bem?' before saying goodbye.

mission.title: Greet a Local
prep: Practice saying 'Bom dia! Tudo bem?'
Practice responding with 'Tudo bem, obrigado. E o senhor/a senhora?'
Practice saying 'Tenha um bom dia!'

variants.note: A common Madeiran way to agree.

cultural_notes.title: Greetings & Presence
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, greetings are more than just words; they are a social ritual. Always respond to "Tudo bem?" with "Tudo bem, obrigado/a" before asking back.

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Greetings
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, as in most of Portugal, a proper greeting is essential before starting any interaction, even a quick one. It's considered polite to always say 'Bom dia', 'Boa tarde', or 'Boa noite' when entering a shop, restaurant, or even just passing someone on a path. Skipping this step can be seen as impolite or abrupt.

cultural_notes.title: 'Tudo bem?' as a Common Check-in
cultural_notes.body: The phrase 'Tudo bem?' (Everything OK?) is more than just a question; it's a common social ritual. It's often used as part of a greeting and is usually responded to positively, even if things aren't perfectly 'bem'. It shows care and acknowledgment, and it's polite to always ask it back to the other person.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Bom dia! Tudo bem?"
Say this phrase from memory: "Tudo bem, obrigado."
Say this phrase from memory: "E você?"
Say this phrase from memory: "Tenha um bom dia!"
Recall the Portuguese for: Good morning
Recall the Portuguese for: Good afternoon
Recall the Portuguese for: Good evening/night
Recall the Portuguese for: Everything OK?
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

dialogues.title: Ordering Coffee and Pastry
dialogues.context: A customer orders an espresso and a pastry at a local café.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia! Queria uma bica e um pastel de nata, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning! I'd like an espresso and a custard tart, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Pois, uma bica e um pastel. É p'ra beber aqui?
lines.translation: Right, an espresso and a tart. Is it to drink here?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Sim, é p'ra beber aqui, obrigado.
lines.translation: Yes, it's to drink here, thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Tá bem. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Okay. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Não, obrigado. A conta, por favor.
lines.translation: No, thank you. The bill, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: São dois euros e cinquenta.
lines.translation: That's two euros and fifty cents.

dialogues.title: Ordering Different Drinks
dialogues.context: A customer orders a 'galão' and an orange juice.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Olá. Queria um galão e um sumo de laranja, por favor.
lines.translation: Hello. I'd like a 'galão' and an orange juice, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Um galão e um sumo. P'ra já!
lines.translation: A 'galão' and a juice. Coming right up!

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Obrigado.
lines.translation: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Aqui tem.
lines.translation: Here you go.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Quanto é?
lines.translation: How much is it?

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Três euros e vinte.
lines.translation: Three euros and twenty cents.

mission.title: Your First Café Order
prep: Review the different types of coffee and pastries you might want to order.
Practice ordering using 'Queria um/uma...' for different items.
Be ready to ask 'Quanto é?' or 'A conta, por favor?' to pay.

variants.text: Queria um sumo de laranja, por favor.
variants.translation: I'd like an orange juice, please.

variants.text: Uma meia de leite, se faz favor.

cultural_notes.title: Ordering in a Café
cultural_notes.body: Ordering coffee in Madeira is specific. A "bica" is your standard espresso. If you want a bit of milk, ask for a "garoto".

cultural_notes.title: Coffee Names in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: While 'café' generally refers to an espresso throughout Portugal, in Madeira, locals often use 'bica' instead. Both terms are understood, but using 'bica' will make you sound more local. Other common coffee types include 'meia de leite' (half coffee, half milk) and 'galão' (a larger milk coffee, served in a tall glass).

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Ordering
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, politeness is highly valued. Always remember to use 'por favor' or 'se faz favor' (both meaning 'please') when making a request. 'Obrigado' (if you're male) or 'Obrigada' (if you're female) for 'thank you' is also essential when receiving your order or the bill.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Queria um café, por favor."
Say this phrase from memory: "Uma bica, se faz favor."
Say this phrase from memory: "Quanto é?"
Say this phrase from memory: "P'ra já!"
Recall the Portuguese for: Espresso
Recall the Portuguese for: Breakfast
Recall the Portuguese for: Custard tart
Recall the Portuguese for: The bill

dialogues.title: Ordering at the café
dialogues.context: A tourist is ordering coffee and a pastry at a café in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Bom dia! O que deseja?
lines.translation: Good morning! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Bom dia. Queria dois cafés e um bolo de arroz, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning. I'd like two coffees and a rice cake, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Dois cafés e um bolo de arroz. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Two coffees and a rice cake. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Não, só isso. Quanto é?
lines.translation: No, just that. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: São três euros e um bocado. Quatro euros, no total.
lines.translation: It's three euros and a bit. Four euros, in total.

dialogues.title: Buying fruit
dialogues.context: A local is buying fruit at the market, asking about prices and quantities.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Diga! Quer o quê?
lines.translation: Yes! What do you want?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia. Queria cinco bananas, mas não muito grandes.
lines.translation: Good morning. I'd like five bananas, but not too big.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Cinco bananas. Certo. E mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Five bananas. Alright. And anything else?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Sim, e três maçãs, por favor, porque gosto muito de maçãs.
lines.translation: Yes, and three apples, please, because I really like apples.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Cinco bananas e três maçãs. São sete euros.
lines.translation: Five bananas and three apples. That's seven euros.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small shop in Madeira, buying some items. You need to ask for quantities and understand prices.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! O que deseja?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! What would you like?

options.text: Bom dia. Queria um sumo e dois bolos.
options.translation: Good morning. I'd like one juice and two cakes.
options.feedback: Great start! You asked for two items using 'e'.

options.translation: I'd like three waters, but small ones.
options.feedback: Good use of 'mas' to specify.

nodes.npc_text: Um sumo e dois bolos. Certo. São cinco euros.
nodes.npc_translation: One juice and two cakes. Right. That's five euros.

options.translation: Five euros? That's a bit much.
options.feedback: You expressed surprise about the price using 'um bocado'.

options.translation: Okay, here you go. Thank you.
options.feedback: Polite and direct ending to the transaction.

nodes.npc_text: Três águas pequenas. Ótimo. São quatro euros.
nodes.npc_translation: Three small waters. Great. That's four euros.

options.text: Quatro euros. E queria mais um café, por favor.
options.translation: Four euros. And I'd like one more coffee, please.
options.feedback: You added another item using 'e'.

options.translation: So, just the waters. Thank you.
options.feedback: You chose to buy only the initial items.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é. Os preços sobem. Quer só um bolo, então?
nodes.npc_translation: That's how it is. Prices go up. Do you want just one cake, then?

options.text: Sim, só um bolo, porque é mais barato.
options.translation: Yes, just one cake, because it's cheaper.
options.feedback: You used 'porque' to explain your choice.

options.text: Não, está bem. Dois bolos e um sumo.
options.translation: No, it's fine. Two cakes and one juice.
options.feedback: You decided to stick with your original order.

nodes.npc_text: Mais um café. Então, três águas e um café. São cinco euros e um bocado.
nodes.npc_translation: One more coffee. So, three waters and one coffee. That's five euros and a bit.

options.text: Ok, são seis euros. Aqui está.
options.translation: Okay, that's six euros. Here you go.
options.feedback: You understood the total and paid.

mission.title: A Shopping List in Funchal
prep: Practice counting from one to ten out loud.
Rehearse ordering two different items using 'e' (e.g., 'dois cafés e um sumo').
Think of a simple reason to use 'porque' (e.g., 'porque é para duas pessoas').
Be ready to ask 'Quanto é?' (How much is it?).

variants.text: Queria um café e uma água.
variants.translation: I'd like one coffee and one water.

variants.text: Queria três sumos, mas só tenho cinco euros.
variants.translation: I'd like three juices, but I only have five euros.

variants.text: Queria quatro bolos, porque gosto muito.
variants.translation: I'd like four cakes, because I like them a lot.

cultural_notes.title: Numbers 1-10 + Connectors
cultural_notes.body: Numbers are the backbone of daily transactions. Connectors like "porque" help you explain your choices, making you sound more natural.

cultural_notes.title: Counting with Fingers in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: When indicating numbers with your fingers in Portugal, especially 1, 2, and 3, it's common to start with your thumb for one, thumb and index for two, and so on. This can sometimes differ from other cultures where the index finger might be 'one'.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'e' for 'and'
cultural_notes.body: The conjunction 'e' (and) is essential for linking items in a list or combining simple ideas. It's often used when ordering multiple items in a shop or café, like 'um café e um bolo' (one coffee and one cake).

dialogues.title: Asking for directions
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a shop assistant for directions to a specific street.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Olá. Onde é a Rua da Carreira?
lines.translation: Hello. Where is Carreira Street?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: É aqui à direita, depois segue sempre em frente e vira na segunda à esquerda.
lines.translation: It's here to the right, then you go straight ahead and turn on the second left.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Não percebi. Pode repetir?
lines.translation: I didn't understand. Can you repeat?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Claro. Vá por aqui, à direita. Depois, siga em frente. Na segunda rua, vira à esquerda.
lines.translation: Of course. Go this way, to the right. Then, go straight. On the second street, turn left.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Ah, percebi. Obrigado!
lines.translation: Ah, I understood. Thank you!

dialogues.title: Ordering a coffee
dialogues.context: A local barista speaks quickly to a tourist trying to order a coffee.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Um café, se faz favor.
lines.translation: A coffee, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: É p'ra beber aqui ou p'ra levar? Com açúcar ou sem? Quer um bolo do caco?
lines.translation: Is it to drink here or to take away? With sugar or without? Do you want a bolo do caco?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Diz? Mais devagar, se faz favor.
lines.translation: Pardon? More slowly, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Ah, desculpe. P'ra beber aqui? Ou p'ra levar?
lines.translation: Oh, sorry. To drink here? Or to take away?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: P'ra beber aqui.
lines.translation: To drink here.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local market in Funchal, trying to buy some fruit. The vendor speaks a bit fast.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Posso ajudar? Temos umas bananas da Madeira ótimas hoje!
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Can I help? We have some great Madeiran bananas today!

options.text: Bom dia. Queria um quilo de bananas, se faz favor.
options.translation: Good morning. I'd like a kilo of bananas, please.
options.feedback: Great start, polite and clear.
A bit direct, but understandable. Try to add 'queria um quilo'.

nodes.npc_text: Um quilo? Ok! Quer também umas maracujás ou umas anonas? Estão fresquinhas!
nodes.npc_translation: A kilo? Okay! Do you also want some passion fruit or some custard apples? They're very fresh!

options.feedback: Excellent, using your escape hatch!
Good, asking for slower speech.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, desculpe! Perguntei se queria maracujás ou anonas. São frutas da ilha.
nodes.npc_translation: Oh, sorry! I asked if you wanted passion fruit or custard apples. They are island fruits.

options.translation: No, thank you. Just the bananas.
options.feedback: Clear and polite refusal.

options.text: Sim, uma maracujá, se faz favor.
options.feedback: Good, you understood and added to your order.

nodes.npc_text: Muito bem. São três euros e cinquenta. Obrigado e volte sempre!
nodes.npc_translation: Very good. That's three euros fifty. Thank you and come back often!

options.feedback: Perfect ending to the interaction.

mission.title: Ask for Repetition at the Market
prep: Imagine you are at a fruit stall in a Madeiran market.
Practice saying 'Diz?' and 'Mais devagar, se faz favor.'
Think about what you might want to buy (e.g., 'um quilo de bananas', 'duas maçãs').

variants.note: Can sound abrupt if not used carefully.

variants.text: Pode dizer de novo, por favor?
variants.translation: Can you say it again, please?

variants.text: Fale mais devagar, se faz favor.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Asking for Repetition
cultural_notes.body: While 'Diz?' or 'Como?' are common informal ways to ask for repetition, adding 'se faz favor' or 'por favor' (please) makes the request more polite and is generally appreciated, especially with strangers or in service situations. 'Não percebi' is a very clear and neutral way to indicate lack of understanding.

cultural_notes.title: Paciência: A Madeiran Trait
cultural_notes.body: The word 'Paciência' (patience) is not just a vocabulary item; it reflects a common attitude in Madeira. People are generally understanding if you need them to speak more slowly or repeat themselves, especially with tourists. Don't be afraid to use your 'escape hatch' phrases.

Say this phrase from memory: "Não percebi."

Say this phrase from memory: "Mais devagar, se faz favor."

Recall the Portuguese for: Understood
Recall the Portuguese for: Slowly
Recall the Portuguese for: Patience

dialogues.title: Asking for the market
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a local for directions to the market.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Com licença, onde fica o mercado?
lines.translation: Excuse me, where is the market?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: O mercado? Pois, é ali. Vire à direita na esquina, depois sempre em frente.
lines.translation: The market? Ah, it's there. Turn right at the corner, then straight ahead.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Sempre em frente?
lines.translation: Straight ahead?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Isso mesmo. É um bocado em baixo.
lines.translation: That's right. It's a bit downhill.

dialogues.title: Finding a pharmacy
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a shop assistant for a pharmacy.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Bom dia. Desculpe, onde fica a farmácia?
lines.translation: Good morning. Excuse me, where is the pharmacy?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Bom dia. A farmácia? Fica em cima, aqui perto.
lines.translation: Good morning. The pharmacy? It's uphill, near here.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Em cima?
lines.translation: Uphill?

lines.speaker: Assistente
lines.text: Sim, vire à esquerda, depois é a primeira à direita. É fácil.
lines.translation: Yes, turn left, then it's the first on the right. It's easy.

roleplay.scenario: You are in Funchal and need to find a bus stop to go to the botanical garden. Ask a local person for directions.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Can I help you?

options.text: Onde fica a paragem de autocarro para o Jardim Botânico?
options.translation: Where is the bus stop for the Botanical Garden?
options.feedback: Great start, asking directly for the bus stop!
You'll get directions to the garden, but not necessarily the bus stop.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, para o Jardim Botânico... Pois, a paragem fica ali, em cima. Vire à direita, depois sempre em frente, na próxima esquina.
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, for the Botanical Garden... Well, the stop is there, uphill. Turn right, then straight ahead, at the next corner.

options.feedback: Good job confirming the directions!
A good question to gauge the walkability.

nodes.npc_text: O Jardim Botânico é um bocado longe, mas o autocarro vai lá. A paragem é ali, em cima.
nodes.npc_translation: The Botanical Garden is a bit far, but the bus goes there. The stop is there, uphill.

options.text: Onde fica a paragem de autocarro?
options.feedback: Good, you clarified for the bus stop specifically.

nodes.npc_text: Isso mesmo. Em cima, à direita, e depois sempre em frente.
nodes.npc_translation: That's right. Uphill, to the right, and then straight ahead.

options.feedback: Polite ending, well done!

nodes.npc_text: É um bocado longe para ir a pé, mas de autocarro é rápido.
nodes.npc_translation: It's a bit far to go on foot, but by bus it's quick.

options.feedback: Polite ending, well done!

nodes.npc_text: Vire à direita, depois sempre em frente, na próxima esquina. É em cima.
nodes.npc_translation: Turn right, then straight ahead, at the next corner. It's uphill.

options.feedback: Polite ending, well done!

mission.title: Find the nearest pharmacy
prep: Practice asking 'Onde fica a farmácia?'
Review 'em cima', 'em baixo', 'à esquerda', 'à direita', 'sempre em frente'.
Remember to say 'Com licença' or 'Desculpe' first.
likely_responses: É em cima, sempre em frente.
É um bocado longe, em baixo.

variants.note: More formal way to say 'Go straight ahead'.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Asking for Directions
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, it's customary to start your request for directions with a polite phrase like 'Com licença' (Excuse me) or 'Desculpe' (Sorry/Excuse me) before asking your question. This shows respect and acknowledges that you're interrupting someone.

cultural_notes.title: Madeiran Directions: Uphill and Downhill
cultural_notes.body: Given Funchal's hilly landscape, directions often include 'em cima' (uphill) and 'em baixo' (downhill). These aren't just about elevation but often imply a general direction relative to the main road or sea. Pay attention to these terms as they are crucial for navigating the city.

Say this phrase from memory: "Vire à esquerda."

dialogues.title: At the café
dialogues.context: A tourist and a local at a café, discussing food and drink.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Então, gosta da bica?
lines.translation: So, do you like the coffee?

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Sim, gosto imenso! É uma maravilha.
lines.translation: Yes, I like it very much! It's wonderful.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Pois é! E o bolo do caco? Acha que é bom?
lines.translation: Yes, it is! And the 'bolo do caco'? Do you think it's good?

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Acho que parece ótimo! Quero provar.
lines.translation: I think it looks great! I want to try it.

dialogues.title: Talking about Madeira
dialogues.context: Two people discussing their impressions of Madeira.

lines.speaker: Friend 1
lines.text: A Madeira é uma maravilha, não é?
lines.translation: Madeira is wonderful, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Friend 2
lines.text: Pois é, gosto imenso daqui! O tempo parece-me ótimo.
lines.translation: Yes, it is, I like it here a lot! The weather seems great to me.

lines.speaker: Friend 1
lines.text: Mesmo! Acha que é um bom lugar para viver?
lines.translation: Exactly! Do you think it's a good place to live?

lines.speaker: Friend 2
lines.text: Parece-me que sim. Imenso sol!
lines.translation: It seems so to me. Lots of sun!

roleplay.scenario: You are at a fruit stall in a local market in Funchal. The vendor offers you a taste of a local fruit.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Quer provar esta fruta? É da Madeira.
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Do you want to try this fruit? It's from Madeira.

options.feedback: A polite way to accept the offer.

nodes.npc_text: Então, o que achou? É bom?
nodes.npc_translation: So, what did you think? Is it good?

options.feedback: Excellent, you expressed strong liking.
Good, a neutral positive opinion.
Great, a strong positive opinion using 'acho que'.

nodes.npc_text: Que bom! É uma das nossas melhores. Quer levar um?
nodes.npc_translation: How good! It's one of our best. Do you want to take one?

options.feedback: You've successfully made a purchase!
Politely declining is also important.

nodes.npc_text: Acha que sim? Temos outras frutas também. Quer provar outra coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Do you think so? We have other fruits too. Do you want to try something else?

options.feedback: You politely declined further offers.
You are open to trying more. Good!

mission.title: Share your opinion on a local dish
prep: Practice saying 'Gosto imenso!', 'Parece-me bem.', and 'Acho que é uma maravilha!'.
Think about something you've tried in Madeira (like 'bolo do caco' or 'poncha').

variants.text: Gosto imenso do bolo do caco.
variants.translation: I really like the 'bolo do caco'.
It seems very good to me.

variants.note: Using 'acho que' to introduce the opinion.

variants.text: O bolo do caco é uma maravilha.

variants.note: Using 'acho que' to introduce the opinion.

cultural_notes.title: Softening opinions with 'Parece-me'
cultural_notes.body: While 'Acho que...' (I think that...) is a direct way to express an opinion, 'Parece-me que...' (It seems to me that...) or simply 'Parece-me bem.' (It seems good to me.) is a slightly softer and often more polite way to share your thoughts, especially when you're not entirely certain or want to be less assertive. It's very common in everyday conversation.

cultural_notes.title: The versatility of 'Pois é!'
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois é!' is a very common and versatile interjection in Portuguese. It can mean 'That's right!', 'Exactly!', 'Indeed!', or simply be a way to acknowledge what someone has said, showing agreement or understanding. It's often used to keep a conversation flowing naturally.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Gosto imenso!"
Say this phrase from memory: "Parece-me bem."
Say this phrase from memory: "É uma maravilha."
Say this phrase from memory: "Pois é!"
Recall the Portuguese for: Very much / A lot
Recall the Portuguese for: Great
Recall the Portuguese for: Wonderful
Recall the Portuguese for: Really / Exactly

dialogues.title: Ordering at a Café
dialogues.context: You are at a local café in Funchal, ordering your breakfast.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Olá! Bom dia! O que deseja?
lines.translation: Hello! Good morning! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: Bom dia. Queria uma bica, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning. I'd like an espresso, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Uma bica. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: An espresso. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: Sim, e um bolo do caco, por favor.
lines.translation: Yes, and a bolo do caco, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Tá bem. É para aqui ou para levar?
lines.translation: Okay. Is it for here or to take away?

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: É para aqui, obrigado.
lines.translation: It's for here, thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Pois. Já vai.
lines.translation: Right. Coming up.

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: A conta, por favor.
lines.translation: The bill, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: São três euros e vinte. Obrigado.
lines.translation: That's three euros twenty. Thank you.

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: Obrigada! Adeus.
lines.translation: Thank you! Goodbye.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Adeus. Volte sempre!
lines.translation: Goodbye. Come again!

dialogues.title: Asking for Directions
dialogues.context: You are on a street in Funchal, trying to find a specific street.

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: Com licença, desculpe. Onde é a Rua da Carreira?
lines.translation: Excuse me, sorry. Where is Rua da Carreira?

lines.speaker: Transeunte
lines.text: A Rua da Carreira? Pois... é um bocado para lá.
lines.translation: Rua da Carreira? Well... it's a bit over there.

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: Ah, tá bem. É muito longe?
lines.translation: Ah, okay. Is it very far?

lines.speaker: Transeunte
lines.text: Não, não. Um bocado a pé. Vai por ali, depois vira à direita...
lines.translation: No, no. A bit on foot. You go that way, then turn right...

lines.speaker: Você
lines.text: Ah, percebi. Muito obrigada! Até já!
lines.translation: Ah, I understand. Thank you very much! See you!

lines.speaker: Transeunte
lines.text: De nada! Adeus.
lines.translation: You're welcome! Goodbye.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local pastelaria (bakery/café) in Funchal, ready to order a quick snack and a drink.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Bom dia! O que vai ser?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! Good morning! What will it be?

options.text: Bom dia. Queria um café, por favor.
options.translation: Good morning. I'd like a coffee, please.
options.feedback: A good start, clear and polite.

options.text: Olá. Um pastel de nata, por favor.
options.translation: Hello. A pastel de nata, please.
options.feedback: Directly ordering, also good.

nodes.npc_text: Um café. Mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: A coffee. Anything else?

options.text: Sim, e um bolo do caco, por favor.
options.translation: Yes, and a bolo do caco, please.
options.feedback: Adding another item, well done.

options.translation: No, thank you. Just the coffee.
options.feedback: Politely declining, simple and effective.

nodes.npc_text: Um pastel de nata. Mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: A pastel de nata. Anything else?

options.text: Sim, e uma bica, por favor.
options.feedback: Successfully added a drink.

options.translation: No, thank you. Just the pastry.
options.feedback: Clear and concise.

nodes.npc_text: Tá bem. É para aqui ou para levar?
nodes.npc_translation: Okay. Is it for here or to take away?

options.feedback: Good, indicating you'll eat in.
Good, indicating you'll take it to go.

nodes.npc_text: Tá bem. É para aqui ou para levar?
nodes.npc_translation: Okay. Is it for here or to take away?

options.feedback: Correct response for eating in.
Correct response for taking it to go.

nodes.npc_text: Fica dois euros e setenta. Obrigado/a.
nodes.npc_translation: That's two euros seventy. Thank you.

options.translation: Here you go. Thank you very much!
options.feedback: Successfully completed the transaction and showed gratitude.
A polite and clear exit.

mission.title: Order and Pay at a Café
prep: Practice greeting the server with 'Bom dia' or 'Olá'.
Rehearse ordering a coffee ('uma bica') and a pastry ('um pastel de nata' or 'um bolo do caco') using 'Queria... por favor'.
Practice asking for the bill: 'A conta, por favor'.
Be ready to say 'Obrigado/a' and 'Adeus' when leaving.
fallback_phrases: Desculpe, não percebi. Pode repetir, por favor?
likely_responses: É para aqui ou para levar?

cultural_notes.title: Bica vs. Café
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, especially in Madeira, if you ask for 'um café', you will almost always receive an espresso. Many locals use the term 'uma bica' interchangeably with 'um café' to refer to an espresso. If you want a longer coffee, like an Americano, you'll need to specify 'um abatanado' or 'um café cheio'.

cultural_notes.title: Politeness in Transactions
cultural_notes.body: Politeness is highly valued in daily interactions. Always use 'por favor' (please) when asking for something and 'obrigado/a' (thank you) when receiving. 'Com licença' (excuse me) is used to get someone's attention or to pass by someone in a crowded space. These small words make a big difference.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Full Week 1 Review"

dialogues.title: Meeting a Local
dialogues.context: A tourist introduces themselves to a local person at a café in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Olá! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Hello! All good?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Olá! Sim, tudo bem, obrigado. Chamo-me Sarah.
lines.translation: Hello! Yes, all good, thank you. My name is Sarah.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Muito prazer, Sarah. Eu sou o Manuel. De onde é que é?
lines.translation: Nice to meet you, Sarah. I'm Manuel. Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Sou da Inglaterra. Estou cá há uma semana.
lines.translation: I'm from England. I've been here for a week.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Ah, pois é! Bem-vinda à Madeira!
lines.translation: Oh, that's right! Welcome to Madeira!

dialogues.title: At the Hotel Reception
dialogues.context: A new guest checks in at a hotel in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Rececionista
lines.text: Boa tarde. Em que posso ajudar?
lines.translation: Good afternoon. How can I help?

lines.speaker: Hóspede
lines.text: Boa tarde. Chamo-me Mark. Tenho uma reserva.
lines.translation: Good afternoon. My name is Mark. I have a reservation.

lines.speaker: Rececionista
lines.text: Certo, senhor Mark. De onde é o senhor?
lines.translation: Right, Mr. Mark. Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Hóspede
lines.text: Sou do Canadá. Mudei-me para cá há pouco tempo.
lines.translation: I'm from Canada. I moved here recently.

lines.speaker: Rececionista
lines.text: Ah, que bom! Bem-vindo ao nosso hotel.
lines.translation: Oh, that's good! Welcome to our hotel.

roleplay.scenario: You are meeting a new neighbour in your building in Funchal. Introduce yourself.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! É novo por cá, certo? Eu sou a Sofia, a sua vizinha.
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! You're new here, right? I'm Sofia, your neighbour.

options.translation: Hello, Sofia. My name is [your name].
options.feedback: Great start! You introduced your name clearly.

nodes.npc_text: Muito prazer, [o seu nome]. De onde é que é?
nodes.npc_translation: Nice to meet you, [your name]. Where are you from?

options.feedback: Good! You stated your origin clearly.
Perfect! Stating your city is also very natural.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, que interessante! E o que faz cá na Madeira?
nodes.npc_translation: Oh, how interesting! And what do you do here in Madeira?

options.feedback: Excellent! You explained your reason for being here.
Well done! A common reason for being in Madeira.
Good! You stated your occupation directly.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é! Que bom tê-lo/a por cá. Se precisar de alguma coisa, diga!
nodes.npc_translation: That's right! It's good to have you here. If you need anything, just say!

options.feedback: Great job! You ended the conversation politely.

mission.title: Introduce Yourself to a Local
prep: Practice saying your name: 'Chamo-me [o seu nome]'.
Practice saying your origin: 'Sou de [o seu país/cidade]'.
Practice saying your reason for being in Madeira: 'Estou cá há [tempo]' OR 'Sou reformado/a' OR 'Trabalho cá'.

cultural_notes.title: Muito prazer vs. Prazer
cultural_notes.body: When introduced to someone, 'Muito prazer' (pronounced 'moo-ee-toh pra-ZER') is the polite and common phrase, meaning 'Nice to meet you' or 'Much pleasure'. While 'Prazer' alone is sometimes used, 'Muito prazer' is generally preferred as it is more complete and formal, especially in a first encounter.

cultural_notes.title: Addressing People: 'Tu' vs. 'O Senhor / A Senhora'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'tu' is used for informal address with friends, family, and peers. For strangers, especially older people or those in service roles (like shopkeepers, receptionists), it's more polite to use the third person singular with 'o senhor' (for a man) or 'a senhora' (for a woman). For example, instead of 'Como te chamas?' (informal), you'd say 'Como se chama o senhor/a senhora?' or simply 'Como se chama?' (using the polite 3rd person form). At L0, it's safer to default to the polite form with strangers until you are invited to use 'tu'.
